events.title: Virtu Ferries Founded (1988 Anniversary)
events.notes: Virtu Ferries was founded on 17 July 1988 in Malta — 38 years of continuous Malta–Sicily service. Use as a heritage/credibility moment around 17 July each year. Recurring brand anniversary. Not a hard sell.

events.title: Saint John Paul II — Flagship Entered Service (March 2019)
events.notes: The flagship Saint John Paul II entered service in March 2019 — 110m, 900 passengers. March is the anniversary window. Good for behind-the-scenes or fleet pride content.

events.title: New Year's Day (Malta)
events.notes: Public holiday in Malta. Good reason-to-book moment for Sicilians wanting to spend New Year in Malta. Promote 4–6 weeks ahead.

events.title: Valletta Baroque Festival
events.notes: Annual baroque music festival in Valletta's historic venues. Cultural depth content. Good for destination spotlight (Why Malta). Appeals to culturally curious travellers.

events.title: Epiphany / La Befana (Italy)
events.notes: La Befana — Italian Epiphany. Major Italian holiday. Good window for Italian market: Sicilians could spend Epiphany in Malta. Promote in late November/early December.

events.title: Valentine's Day
events.notes: Romantic travel opportunity. 'Spend Valentine's in Malta / in Sicily.' Works for both markets. Lead with the SNF offer (out Saturday night, back Sunday morning) if running. Promote from late January.

events.title: Carnival (Malta)
events.notes: Maltese Carnival — colourful floats and costumes in Valletta and Nadur (Gozo has its own adult carnival). Great visual content. For Italian market: 'Malta's Carnival — wilder than you'd expect.' Promote 4 weeks out.

events.title: St Paul's Shipwreck — Festa San Pawl (Malta)
events.notes: Maltese public holiday celebrating the shipwreck of St Paul in Malta. Rich cultural/heritage moment for English market. Processions in Valletta.

events.title: St Joseph's Day (Malta & Italy)
events.notes: Public holiday in both Malta and Italy. Family-oriented moment. Shared cultural anchor for both markets.

events.title: Freedom Day (Malta)
events.notes: Maltese public holiday — marks the withdrawal of British forces in 1979. National pride moment. English market audience.

events.title: Good Friday (Malta)
events.notes: Good Friday is a major day of solemn processions in Malta (Rabat, Valletta, Żejtun). Strong visual/cultural content for English market. Italian travellers coming to Malta for Holy Week.

events.title: Easter Weekend
events.notes: Easter Sunday + Easter Monday (Pasquetta). Peak travel window for both markets. Maltese going to Sicily, Sicilians coming to Malta. Start promoting 6 weeks out (mid-February).

events.title: Liberation Day (Italy)
events.notes: Festa della Liberazione — Italian national holiday. Long weekend opportunity for Sicilians to cross to Malta. April 25 + May 1 sometimes creates a week-long bridge.

events.title: Infiorata di Noto (Sicily)
events.notes: Noto's Via Nicolaci carpeted in elaborate flower designs. One of Sicily's most photogenic events. Strong visual content for English market (VF Recommends Sicily). Dates approximate — third weekend of May.

events.title: Workers' Day / May Day (Malta & Italy)
events.notes: Public holiday in both Malta and Italy. Long weekend opportunity. Good reason-to-book post for both markets.

events.title: Summer Season Opens
events.notes: May marks the start of Mediterranean travel season. Sea is warming up, temperatures are ideal, crowds not yet at peak. Key content: 'beat the summer rush', 'May in Sicily/Malta before the crowds'. Start promoting in March.

events.title: Republic Day (Italy)
events.notes: Festa della Repubblica — Italian public holiday. Long weekend opportunity for Sicilians crossing to Malta.

events.title: Sette Giugno (Malta)
events.notes: Maltese national day commemorating the 1919 bread riots. National pride/heritage moment for English market.

events.title: Festa Season (Malta)
events.notes: Maltese village festa season runs June–September. Band marches, fireworks, decorations, food. Every weekend a different village celebrates its patron saint. Rich visual content — authentic Maltese culture. Good for VF Recommends Malta pillar for Italian market.

events.title: Feast of St Peter & St Paul (Malta)
events.notes: Public holiday in Malta. Celebrated with festas across Malta — Marsaxlokk and Nadur especially. Summer festa season in full swing.

events.title: Isle of MTV Malta
events.notes: Isle of MTV — free outdoor concert in Malta (usually July, Floriana or Valletta). One of the biggest free concerts in Europe. Massive draw for young Sicilian and Italian travellers. Date TBC — confirm when announced.

events.title: Malta Jazz Festival
events.notes: Annual jazz festival at the Grand Harbour, Valletta. 3 evenings of international acts. Strong cultural content for both markets. Dates approximate — confirm when announced.

events.title: Peak Summer
events.notes: Peak summer travel season. Highest booking volumes. Promote availability, car booking reminder, schedule content. Ferragosto (Aug 15) is the pinnacle. Content should feel summer-owned, not summer-announcing.

events.title: Assumption Day / Ferragosto (Malta & Italy)
events.notes: Ferragosto — Italy's biggest public holiday. Peak summer travel. Biggest potential travel spike for Italian market. For English market: festas in Malta on Aug 15 (Mqabba, Gudja, Attard, Mosta). Promote from late June.

events.title: End of Summer / Back to School
events.notes: The crowd thins. September is golden — warm sea, emptier streets, lower prices. Position as 'the real summer'. Good content angle for both markets.

events.title: Victory Day (Malta)
events.notes: Maltese public holiday — commemorates the end of the Great Siege of 1565 and WWII siege. Heritage moment for English market. Regatta in Grand Harbour.

events.title: Independence Day (Malta)
events.notes: Malta's Independence Day (1964). National pride moment. Good for English market identity content.

events.title: Etna Wine Harvest Season (Sicily)
events.notes: Grape harvest season on Mount Etna — Nerello Mascalese, the prized Etna Rosso. Wineries open for harvest events. Great VF Recommends Sicily content for English market. Pair with autumn crossings.

events.title: Marsala Wine Harvest (Sicily)
events.notes: Marsala and western Sicily harvest season. Pairs with autumn travel narrative for English market. 'Sicily in harvest' is a strong editorial pillar.

events.title: Autumn in the Mediterranean
events.notes: October in Malta and Sicily is warm and quiet. Wine harvest season in Sicily. Food festivals. Great content for VF Recommends / Sicily Experience. Position autumn as the insider's choice.

events.title: Notte Bianca (Malta)
events.notes: Valletta's annual cultural night festival. Museums, galleries, performances open late and free. Autumn travel hook. Promote for Italian market as reason to cross to Malta in October. Date approximate.

events.title: All Saints Day / Ognissanti (Italy)
events.notes: Ognissanti — Italian public holiday. Long weekend travel opportunity. Malta in autumn: warm, quieter, golden light. Strong sell for Italian market.

events.title: Immaculate Conception (Malta & Italy)
events.notes: Public holiday in both Malta and Italy. Marks the start of the Christmas period. Early December travel opportunity for both markets.

events.title: Republic Day (Malta)
events.notes: Maltese Republic Day. National moment for English market.

events.title: Christmas & New Year Travel
events.notes: Christmas travel window. Malta Christmas market (Valletta), lights, festive atmosphere. Promote from mid-November. Key hooks: 'Spend Christmas in Malta', 'A Sicilian New Year'.

events.title: Christmas Day (Malta & Italy)
events.notes: Christmas Day. Promote Christmas travel from mid-November.

events.title: St Stephen's Day (Italy)
events.notes: Santo Stefano — Italian public holiday. Post-Christmas travel opportunity for Italian market.

events.title: International Women's Day
events.notes: Celebrate strong women — Virtu Ferries crew, Maltese & Sicilian women, travel inspiration.

events.title: Earth Day
events.notes: Our planet, our sea. Sustainability angle — clean oceans, responsible travel.

events.notes: Jazz culture in Malta and Sicily. Malta Jazz Festival connection.

events.notes: Critical day for a maritime brand — celebrate the Mediterranean, ocean conservation.

Living up north — Bugibba, Mellieħa, St Paul's? Skip the drive south. Sail straight from Bugibba to Gozo, from €6.50 (€2.00 for Gozo Residents).

March to October, multiple times a day.
content_posts.visual_direction: Single image: aerial of Bugibba waterfront with route line drawn to Mġarr, brand blue overlay with key fare.
content_posts.title: From the north, in 30 minutes.
content_posts.notes: Bugibba route awareness for north-Malta audience.

content_posts.caption:
Going home shouldn't cost a fortune. Gozo Residents travel Valletta–Gozo for €2.25 one way — just show your Gozitan ID or residence permit at the booth.

€2.00 from Bugibba, €2.25 from Sliema. Welcome aboard.
content_posts.visual_direction: Single image: warm portrait of a Gozitan family at the booth, fare card overlay '€2.25 — Gozo Residents'.
content_posts.title: Gozitan? €2.25 a crossing.
content_posts.notes: Residents fare reminder. Builds local affinity.

content_posts.caption:
Yes, free. The Sliema ↔ Bugibba leg is included with your Tallinja card. Tap, board, sail.

(Add the Gozo leg from either side at standard fares — still the fastest way across.)
content_posts.visual_direction: Single image: hand tapping a Tallinja card at the boarding gate, big 'FREE' lockup in brand red.
content_posts.title: Tallinja card holders ride free.
content_posts.notes: Highlights Tallinja partnership for Sliema-Bugibba shuttle.

content_posts.caption:
Patients travelling to a hospital appointment travel free of charge on the Sliema ↔ Bugibba ↔ Gozo route — both ways.

Bring your appointment confirmation to the booth. We've got you.
content_posts.visual_direction: Single image: calm, service-led photography (boarding ramp, friendly staff). Subtle text 'Patient travel: free with appointment confirmation'.
content_posts.title: Hospital appointment? Sail free.
content_posts.notes: Service-led / community message. Tone: warm, factual, never promotional.

content_posts.caption: The 18:45 from Valletta lands in Gozo at 19:30. Dinner with a sea view, then an easy 22:30 back. Or stay till the 00:30. ⛴️
content_posts.visual_direction: Reel (15s): golden-hour timelapse — Valletta departure → crossing wake → Mġarr arrival → restaurant scene → return ferry. Time stamps overlaid on key beats.
content_posts.title: Out of Valletta by 18:45, on Gozo by 19:30.
content_posts.notes: Evening commute / dinner-out angle.

content_posts.caption:
Friday 18:45 out → Saturday in Xlendi & Dwejra → Sunday lunch in Mġarr → 21:30 back home.

We sail every leg. Swipe for the full timetable. ⏬
content_posts.visual_direction: 5-card weekend itinerary carousel. Card 1 hook, cards 2-4 day-by-day with location photos, card 5 schedule cheat sheet pulled from the brochure.
content_posts.title: Friday-to-Sunday in Gozo.
content_posts.notes: Weekend planner — premium UGC-style imagery if available.

content_posts.caption:
Children 4–10: €3.00. Infants under 3: free. Pile the family in, take the 09:45 from Valletta, be home for bedtime.

No car? Even better — every port is bus-connected.
content_posts.visual_direction: Single image: candid family on the deck, kids pointing at the harbour. Fare card overlay 'Children €3 · Infants free'.
content_posts.title: Family Sunday on Gozo.
content_posts.notes: Family Sunday angle.

content_posts.caption: University and MCAST students travel for €4.50 from Valletta, €3.50 from Bugibba, €4.50 from Sliema. Show your student ID at the booth. Friday in Gozo? Easy.
content_posts.visual_direction: Single image: student-aged passenger leaning on the rail, casual + sun-drenched. Fare lockup in brand colours.
content_posts.title: Student card. Half-price seas.
content_posts.notes: Student fare. Tone: friendly, no jargon.

content_posts.caption:
00:30 out of Valletta. 23:30 back from Gozo. Every Friday and Saturday — every weekend until October.

Who's on the late one? 🌙
content_posts.visual_direction: Reel (10s): night-time deck POV, Mġarr harbour lights, schedule overlay '00:30 / 23:30 · DAILY May–Sep' on the final beat.
content_posts.title: Last ferry, best ferry.
content_posts.notes: Late-night weekend angle. Reinforces summer-daily messaging from May 1.

content_posts.caption:
Senior Citizens travel for €3.00 one-way on every route — Valletta, Sliema, or Bugibba. Just show your ID at the booth.

Mid-week visit to the family in Gozo? Sorted.
content_posts.visual_direction: Single image: warm portrait of an older couple boarding. Fare lockup '€3 — Senior Citizens'.
content_posts.title: Senior fare: €3.00.
content_posts.notes: Senior fare. Tone: respectful, never patronising.

content_posts.caption: 45 minutes from Valletta. 30 from Bugibba. 75 from Sliema. No queues at Ċirkewwa. No backed-up roads. Just sea, then Gozo.
content_posts.visual_direction: Single image: hero shot of vessel at full speed, low angle, brand-blue gradient. Comparative time-stamps as small lockups.
content_posts.title: The fastest way to Gozo.
content_posts.notes: Core USP message — speed vs Ċirkewwa.

content_posts.caption:
Long weekend coming up. Book your Gozo crossing early — the 09:45 and 10:45 from Valletta fill fast on public holidays.

(Full timetable in bio.)
content_posts.visual_direction: Single image: harbour at sunrise, calendar-style overlay 'Sette Giugno · 7 June'. Brand red accent.
content_posts.title: Sette Giugno weekend: extra crossings.
content_posts.notes: Holiday weekend lookahead. Keep informational, not pushy.

content_posts.caption:
Thank you for sailing with us this month. A few highlights from the crossings, the sunsets, and you. ❤️

June is going to be a busy one — see you onboard.
content_posts.visual_direction: Reel (20s): UGC-style monthly montage — departures, deck shots, Gozo scenery, passenger smiles. Soft acoustic track.
content_posts.title: May at sea.
content_posts.notes: Month wrap. Use real passenger UGC if rights cleared, else internal stock.

copywriter_rules.content:
THE VOICE IN ONE LINE
Short. Confident. Specific. Never explains itself.

WHAT THIS VOICE DOES
- One idea per caption. That is enough.
- Ends on a statement, not a question.
- Specific over general. Always.
- Confident without describing. Show, don't enthuse.
- Never tells the audience what to feel.
- Rhythm matters. Read it aloud. If it doesn't land, it isn't done.
- Maximum 3 short paragraphs. Usually fewer.

WHAT TO NEVER DO
- Never write more than 3 short paragraphs.
- Never open with a scene-setting sentence. ("May is one of those months...", "There's something about this time of year..." are banned.)
- Never explain why Sicily or Malta is good. State the fact, let the destination carry it.
- Never use atmospheric filler. Banned: "the light is something else entirely", "the heat is building", "the crowds haven't arrived yet", "the air is different", "something about this season", "there's nowhere quite like".
- Never sound like a tourism board. If it could appear in a travel brochure, rewrite it.
- Never use hashtags. Not one.
- Never use an em dash with spaces (" — " or "— "). Use a comma, line break, or full stop instead.
- Never write "the schedule is live", "just dropped", or "now available" — the timetable is always there.

HARD RULES
- No hashtags ever.
- One exclamation mark maximum per caption, only when earned.
- CTAs only when they add something. Woven into the caption — never as a standalone line.
- Sentence length: vary it. Short punches matter. So does rhythm.
- Do not use occasions such as Mother’s Day, Father’s Day, or Christmas for promotional purposes; they should be acknowledged only with well wishes.

WEEKLY SCHEDULE — SPECIFIC STANDARD
The weekly schedule runs every Saturday. Caption frame: here's what's crossing this week, pick your day.
Tone: informative but human. Flexibility and frequency are the message.
Open with a calendar emoji. End with the timetable link.
Never frame it as an announcement or a launch.

OFFER COPY
Lead with the human benefit, not the price.
"€63.60" is not a headline. Build to the number.
Always direct to virtuferries.com for booking.

NEVER USE THESE WORDS
paradise, breathtaking, unforgettable, hidden gem, postcard-perfect, magical, stunning, incredible, vibrant, bustling

THE VOICE IN ONE LINE
Calm, plain, helpful. Sound like the crew, not the marketing team.

WHAT THIS VOICE DOES
- One idea per caption. No second thought tacked on.
- Leads with the practical thing: the date, the route, the time, the change.
- Plain English. Factual over poetic. Helpful over clever.
- Confident without flourish. State it once and move on.
- Treats the audience as adults who already know Gozo is worth visiting.
- Rhythm matters. If it sounds like a leaflet, rewrite it.
- 1-2 short sentences for service posts. 2-3 for spotlights. Never more.

POST STRUCTURE — applies to every post type unless overridden
- Every post starts with a SHORT BOLD HEADLINE on its own line.
- Headlines use Unicode Sans-Serif Bold (e.g. "𝗦𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗖𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗱"), NOT all caps, NOT markdown asterisks.
- After the headline: blank line, then 1-2 short sentence body, then blank line, then CTA line.
- CTA line uses an emoji (👉 for action, 🌐 for a link) and the brand domain in CamelCase: GozoHighspeed.com.
- Service Update REMOVALS are the exception: no URL, no booking link. Service Update ADDITIONS get a CTA.
- Fleet Highlights are a FULL exception to this structure: NO bold headline, NO multi-block layout, NO CTA, NO URL, NO mention of GozoHighspeed.com. Just one short sentence-case line, often trailing off with "…" or ending with a single emoji like 🌊. The image carries the post.

WHAT TO NEVER DO
- Never open with hype: "exciting news", "we're thrilled", "big announcement", "did you know".
- Never use ALL CAPS for emphasis on any word. Use Unicode Sans-Serif Bold instead.
- Never use emojis in the body opening sentence (only in the headline flag and CTA line).
- Never use exclamation marks on a Service Update or timetable. Tone is calm, not loud.
- Never use tourist-brochure language. Banned: "hidden gem", "must-see", "paradise", "breathtaking", "magical", "stunning", "unforgettable", "vibrant".
- Never invent FALSE urgency. Real availability cues like "selling fast", "last call", "before it's too late" are fine on booking-type posts because they reflect real seat scarcity — but never on Service Update posts.
- Never use hashtags.
- Never use an em dash with spaces (" — " or "— "). Use a comma, line break, or full stop instead.
- Never describe Gozo. Anchor it with a real, specific detail (a place, a sound, a time of day).

HARD RULES
- No hashtags ever.
- The brand domain is always written GozoHighspeed.com (CamelCase). Never gozohighspeed.com, never www.gozohighspeed.com.
- Date format: weekday + ordinal day + month, e.g. "Thursday 30th April". Multiple dates: "April 20th, 25th & 30th".
- Time format: 24-hour, e.g. "07:45".
- No exclamation marks on Service Updates, timetables, booking reminders, fares, or general announcements. Spotlights and fleet highlights may use one, only when earned.
- CTAs are their own line at the end, prefixed with 👉 or 🌐. Service notices have no CTA line.
- Sentence length: short. Vary cadence. Read it aloud before posting.
- Do not use occasions such as Mother's Day, Father's Day, or Christmas for promotional purposes; acknowledge with well wishes only.

SERVICE UPDATE STANDARD
Covers any change to the schedule — REMOVALS (cancellations, suspensions) AND ADDITIONS (extra sailings, new trips). One post type, two polarities.
Headline is a flag, not a summary. Polarity determines the emoji:
  - REMOVALS: "🔴 𝗜𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁: 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗨𝗽𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗲"
  - ADDITIONS: "🟢 𝗘𝘅𝘁𝗿𝗮 𝗦𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗔𝗱𝗱𝗲𝗱" or "🟢 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗨𝗽𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗲"
The specific change goes in the body, not the headline.
Body opens with the reason ("Due to ..."), then names the change and the dates.
Closing depends on the type:
  - REMOVAL — Planned (events, scheduled work): practical planning tip, e.g. "you can book online up to 30 minutes before departure to guarantee your seat".
  - REMOVAL — Unplanned (weather, mechanical, sea conditions): "Updates to follow."
  - ADDITION: a booking CTA, e.g. "👉 Book your seat: GozoHighspeed.com".
NEVER include a URL on a REMOVAL post. ADDITIONS get a CTA — extra capacity needs a booking nudge.
Tone: calm and informative. Never apologetic or dramatic on removals. Never hyped on additions.

TIMETABLE STANDARD
State the dates the schedule covers and the crossing frequency in plain terms. Direct to GozoHighspeed.com for the full schedule. Never frame it as "just dropped" or "now live" — it has always been there.

BOOKING REMINDER STANDARD
Lead with the practical reason to book now (the season, the date, the peak window, the seat scarcity). One nudge, no false pressure.

FARES & OFFERS STANDARD
Lead with the value the rider gets, then the price. Plain and transparent. Build to the number, never headline it.

DESTINATION OR EVENT SPOTLIGHT STANDARD
Open with one real, specific sensory detail or moment in Gozo — not a generic adjective. Anchor it back to the 35-minute crossing.

NEVER USE THESE WORDS
paradise, breathtaking, unforgettable, hidden gem, postcard-perfect, magical, stunning, incredible, vibrant, bustling, exciting news, we're thrilled, big announcement

content_ideas.title: Three Sicilies in one day
content_ideas.body:
Some days are made for layers.

Start in Modica with a coffee and a square of chocolate.
Head up to Ragusa Ibla for slow streets and long shadows.
Finish in Scicli at aperitivo time — limestone glowing, locals exactly where they should be.

This corner of Sicily doesn’t shout.
It just stays with you.

content_ideas.title: Catania, before it wakes up
content_ideas.body:
Catania at 8am is a different city.
Fish market noise building. Espresso at the counter. Black lava stone under your feet.

Do the simple route:
Duomo → Pescheria → Via Etnea → a pistachio pastry you’ll remember.

If you want Sicily with edge (and proper food), this is your sign. ☀️

content_ideas.title: The Sicily beach that feels private
content_ideas.body:
San Lorenzo is the kind of beach you tell people about… carefully.
Soft sand, clear water, and that south-coast calm where time stops arguing with you.

Go early.
Bring something cold.
Stay until the light turns honey.

Sicily does summer properly.

content_ideas.title: Gozo, on your schedule
content_ideas.body:
Need a fast way to get to Gozo without building your day around the trip?

Gozo Highspeed runs frequent sailings between Valletta and Mġarr (Gozo), year-round—so you can plan the rest of your day first, then choose your crossing.

content_ideas.title: North Malta to Gozo in 30 minutes
content_ideas.body:
Starting from the north? Bugibba ↔ Gozo is our shortest crossing.

Seasonal (March–October), direct to Mġarr (Gozo) in around 30 minutes—handy for day trips and quick visits.

content_ideas.title: Sliema to Gozo, with a stop at Bugibba
content_ideas.body:
If you’re coming from Sliema, you can ride the seasonal route to Gozo with a call at Bugibba on the way.

Sliema → Bugibba → Gozo runs daily from March–October, with multiple departures—simple connections, one operator, one journey.

content_ideas.title: Valletta ↔ Gozo: simple, direct, year-round
content_ideas.body:
Need a straightforward way to get to Gozo from Malta?

Our Valletta ↔ Gozo service runs year-round, direct—up to around 15 sailings each way per day. Check the timetable, pick your crossing, and you’re on your way.

content_ideas.title: From the north: 30 minutes to Gozo
content_ideas.body:
If you’re starting from the north of Malta, Bugibba ↔ Gozo is the shortest crossing.

Seasonal (March–October), direct, and around 30 minutes—ideal for day-trips and easy commutes.

content_ideas.title: Sliema → Bugibba → Gozo, one easy route
content_ideas.body:
Starting from Sliema? This route calls at Bugibba before continuing to Gozo.

Sliema ↔ Bugibba is around 45 minutes, Bugibba ↔ Gozo around 30 minutes, and end-to-end about 75 minutes (seasonal, March–October). Plan your crossing and travel light.

voice_profiles.tone: Calm, plain, helpful. Same crew voice for both bad news (cancellations) and good news (added trips). Never apologetic, never overhyped.
voice_profiles.length: Bold flag headline + 1-2 sentence body opening with the reason + closing line (planning tip, 'Updates to follow', or CTA — depends on polarity). Three short blocks separated by blank lines.
voice_profiles.opening:
Sans-Serif Bold flag headline. POLARITY DETERMINES THE EMOJI:
- REMOVALS (cancellation, suspension, schedule reduction): '🔴 𝗜𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁: 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗨𝗽𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗲'
- ADDITIONS (extra sailings, new trips, expanded service): '🟢 𝗘𝘅𝘁𝗿𝗮 𝗦𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗔𝗱𝗱𝗲𝗱' or '🟢 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗨𝗽𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗲'
The specific change goes in the body, never in the headline.
voice_profiles.cta:
POLARITY DETERMINES THE CLOSE:
- REMOVALS: NO CTA, NO URL, NO booking link. Close instead with either:
  • Planned (events, scheduled work): practical planning tip, e.g. 'You can book online up to 30 minutes before departure to guarantee your seat.'
  • Unplanned (weather, mechanical, sea conditions): 'Updates to follow.'
- ADDITIONS: Close with a CTA inviting the booking, e.g. '👉 Book your seat: GozoHighspeed.com'.
voice_profiles.avoid: Apologies ('we're sorry', 'apologies for the inconvenience'), drama ('unfortunately', 'we regret'), hype on additions ('exciting news', 'big announcement', 'we're thrilled'), exclamation marks, ALL CAPS, hashtags. Never include a URL on a removal/cancellation post. Don't promise specific resumption times unless confirmed. Don't add inventory urgency to additions ('selling fast', 'before it fills up') — let the booking link do the work.
voice_profiles.anchor_example:
REMOVAL EXAMPLE:
🔴 𝗜𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁: 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗨𝗽𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗲

Due to high winds, all sailings on Friday 12th May are cancelled.

Updates to follow.

---

ADDITION EXAMPLE:
🟢 𝗘𝘅𝘁𝗿𝗮 𝗦𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗔𝗱𝗱𝗲𝗱

For the May Day long weekend, extra crossings are added on Saturday 3rd & Sunday 4th May.

👉 Book your seat: GozoHighspeed.com

voice_profiles.tone: Casual, observational, lightly playful. Sounds like an Instagram caption from a fan of the boats, not a marketing department. Atmospheric, sometimes personified ('she's already making waves'). The image leads — the caption is a one-line quip that frames the moment.
voice_profiles.length: ONE short line. Sentence fragment is fine. Often trails off with '…' or '..' or ends with a single emoji like 🌊. NO bold headline, NO multi-block structure, NO blank lines. Two short lines max if absolutely needed.
voice_profiles.opening:
Plain sentence case. NO Sans-Serif Bold formatting. NO flag emoji at the start. The line IS the post — there's no headline-then-body structure here.

NOUN POLICY: NEVER call them 'boat' or 'ship'. Refer to the vessel as: a ferry, a vessel, the fleet, or 'she'/'her' (personified). NEVER invent or assume a vessel name. Only use a vessel name if the brief explicitly gives one — most posts don't name the vessel at all.

COHERENCE: The line must read as ONE complete thought, not two fragments stitched with 'and'. BAD: 'mid-channel, and she's already on her way back 🌊' — two half-ideas glued together; neither stands alone, the connection makes no logical sense. GOOD: 'Smooth sailing into the weekend like…' — one whole observation.

OBSERVABILITY: Describe what the image shows, not what came before or after. AVOID temporal narrative the photo can't carry: 'already on her way back', 'returning from', 'after a long day', 'just dropped off'. Stick to what's visible: the place, the light, the moment.

DON'T TEMPLATE THE ANCHORS: The construction 'and she's already X' worked once because of specific context (a new vessel + the natural metaphor 'making waves'). Do NOT reuse it as a formula. Vary the construction every time.

SPECIFICITY: Anchor to a real GHS detail. Strongest anchors (in order of preference):
1. LOCATION — Sliema, Bugibba, Mġarr, the Sliema waterfront, mid-channel.
2. MOMENT — golden hour, the weekend, crossover, first light, last crossing of the day.
3. PERSONIFICATION — used sparingly, with something genuinely observable (not narrative).
4. VESSEL NAME — only if explicitly given in the brief.

It is often best to skip a vessel noun entirely. If the line could be slapped on any ferry photo anywhere in the world, it's too generic. Don't lead with a bare plural noun ('two ferries passing') unless paired with a specific moment or place.

Examples of acceptable openings:
- 'Smooth sailing into the weekend like…'
- 'Sailing into style at the Port of Valletta'
- 'The fastest fleet on the waters'
- 'The Sliema waterfront just got a new regular .. and she's already making waves 🌊'
voice_profiles.cta: NONE. NEVER include 👉, NEVER include a 🌐 link line, NEVER write 'GozoHighspeed.com' anywhere in the post, NEVER use 'book', 'reserve', 'plan your', or any call to action. End on the line itself. This is not a sales post — it's a showcase. The image does the work.
voice_profiles.avoid:
Bold headlines (Sans-Serif Bold or otherwise). Multi-block structure with blank lines. Spec-sheet language ('350 seats', '35 knots', capacity numbers, deck counts). Boastful labels ('our pride', 'flagship', 'state-of-the-art'). Generic adjectives ('amazing', 'incredible', 'stunning'). CTAs, links, booking nudges, the brand domain. Don't over-explain — let the image carry it.

BANNED NOUNS: 'boat', 'ship'. Use vessel, ferry, fleet, 'she', or named ONLY if the brief gives the name.

INVENTED NAMES: NEVER invent or assume a vessel name. If the brief doesn't say which vessel, don't name one — describe the place, the moment, or use 'she'.

INCOHERENT FRAGMENTS: never stitch two half-thoughts with 'and' just because each contains an approved element. Every line must read as ONE single coherent observation. If you wouldn't say it out loud as a normal sentence, rewrite it.

NARRATIVE INFERENCE: never describe a state the image can't show. Banned: 'already on her way back', 'returning from', 'after a long day', 'just dropped off', 'about to depart' (unless the photo clearly shows departure).

TEMPLATING ANCHORS: never reuse the construction 'and she's already...' from the anchor examples — it's a one-off, not a formula. Vary the construction.

GENERIC CAPTIONS: anything that could apply to any ferry company anywhere. 'Two boats / ships / ferries passing' on its own is generic. Anchor to GHS specifics — location, moment, or personification.
voice_profiles.anchor_example:
The fastest fleet on the waters

---

Smooth sailing into the weekend like…

---

Sailing into style at the Port of Valletta

---

The Sliema waterfront just got a new regular .. and she's already making waves 🌊

voice_profiles.tone: Direct, useful, mildly urgent — but never pushy. Use real seat-availability cues only ('selling fast', 'last call', 'before it's too late'). No false drama.
voice_profiles.length: Bold headline + 1-2 sentence body + CTA line. Three short blocks separated by blank lines.
voice_profiles.opening: Sans-Serif Bold headline naming the specific scarcity — a sailing time, a route, a date, or a peak window. Triggers: peak weekends, specific times of day filling up, event dates. No 🔴 emoji on booking reminders.
voice_profiles.cta: Own line at the end, prefixed with 👉. The verb can vary to fit the post: 'Book Now', 'Reserve your seat', 'Secure your spot', 'Save your seat' all work. Always ends with the domain in CamelCase: GozoHighspeed.com.
voice_profiles.avoid: Exclamation marks, ALL CAPS, false drama, countdown emojis (⏰ ⏳), vague 'book now' without context, hashtags. NEVER frame seats as inventory/stock — banned phrasings: 'before it's gone', 'before it fills up', 'before it sells out', 'don't miss', 'limited time', 'hurry', 'do not miss out'. Time-based urgency about the journey itself IS allowed because it's true to the service: 'before it's too late', 'last call'. If the body already says 'selling fast', do NOT add a second urgency line — let the CTA do the rest.

𝗟𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝗖𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝟬𝟳:𝟰𝟱 𝘁𝗼 𝗩𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗮
